chunk_summary: This section discusses the use of ICDS entitlements and nutrition counselling norms for managing moderate acute malnutrition (MAM) in children, focusing on supplementary feeding regimens through anganwadi services under Mission Poshan and ICDS guidelines.
learning_objectives: Understand the role of ICDS entitlements in supplementary feeding for children with MAM.
Learn the nutritional norms and daily energy/protein targets for malnourished children under Mission Poshan guidelines.
Identify challenges in supplementary feeding, such as inconsistent receipt or preparation issues.
Explore the WHO concept of supplementary feeding as a top-up to daily energy needs.
key_facts: Supplementary feeding under ICDS is delivered for a minimum of 300 days per year through anganwadi services.
Daily nutrition norms for children 6–72 months: ~500 kcal and 12–15 g protein; for severely malnourished children: ~800 kcal and 20–25 g protein.
Supplementary nutrition rules (2017) include enhanced provisions for malnourished children, such as energy and protein targets for THR and HCM.
WHO recommends supplementary energy contributions of 40–60% of total daily energy needs during recovery.
Mission Poshan guidance emphasizes bridging the gap between recommended dietary allowances and average intake.
reference_quote: Mission Poshan guidance lists daily norms for children 6–72 months of about 500 kcal and 12–15 g protein, with higher norms of about 800 kcal and 20–25 g protein for children classified as severely malnourished.
